title: The Spelling Bee at Angels
content:
آ آ آ آ Waltz in, waltz in, ye little kids, and gather round my knee,
آ آ آ آ And drop them books and first pot-hooks, and hear a yarn from me.
آ آ آ آ I kin not sling a fairy tale of Jinnys fierce and wild,
آ آ آ آ For I hold it is unchristian to deceive a simple child;
آ آ آ آ But as from school yer driftinï؟½ by, I thowt yeï؟½d like to hear
آ آ آ آ Of a ï؟½Spelling Beeï؟½ at Angels that we organized last year.

آ آ آ آ It warnï؟½t made up of gentle kids, of pretty kids, like you,
آ آ آ آ But gents ez hed their regï؟½lar growth, and some enough for two.
آ آ آ آ There woz Lanky Jim of Sutterï؟½s Fork and Bilson of Lagrange,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½Pistol Bob,ï؟½ who wore that day a knife by way of change.
آ آ آ آ You start, you little kids, you think these are not pretty names,
آ آ آ آ But each had a man behind it, and my name is Truthful James.

آ آ آ آ There was Poker Dick from Whisky Flat, and Smith of Shooterï؟½s Bend,
آ آ آ آ And Brown of Calaveras which I want no better friend;
آ آ آ آ Three-fingered Jack yes, pretty dears, three fingers you have five.
آ آ آ آ Clapp cut off two itï؟½s singï؟½lar, too, that Clapp ainï؟½t now alive.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas very wrong indeed, my dears, and Clapp was much to blame;
آ آ آ آ Likewise was Jack, in after-years, for shootinï؟½ of that same.

آ آ آ آ The nights was kinder lengtheninï؟½ out, the rains had jest begun,
آ آ آ آ When all the camp came up to Peteï؟½s to have their usual fun;
آ آ آ آ But we all sot kinder sad-like around the bar-room stove
آ آ آ آ Till Smith got up, permiskiss-like, and this remark he hove:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tharï؟½s a new game down in Frisco, that ez far ez I can see
آ آ آ آ Beats euchre, poker, and van-toon, they calls the ï؟½Spellinï؟½ Bee.ï؟½ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Then Brown of Calaveras simply hitched his chair and spake,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Poker is good enough for me,ï؟½ and Lanky Jim sez, ï؟½Shake!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And Bob allowed he warnï؟½t proud, but he ï؟½must say right thar
آ آ آ آ That the man who tackled euchre hed his education squar.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ This brought up Lenny Fairchild, the schoolmaster, who said
آ آ آ آ He knew the game, and he would give instructions on that head.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½For instance, take some simple word,ï؟½ sez he, ï؟½like ï؟½separate:ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Now who can spell it?ï؟½ Dog my skin, ef thar was one in eight.
آ آ آ آ This set the boys all wild at once. The chairs was put in row,
آ آ آ آ And at the head was Lanky Jim, and at the foot was Joe,
آ آ آ آ And high upon the bar itself the schoolmaster was raised,
آ آ آ آ And the bar-keep put his glasses down, and sat and silent gazed.

آ آ آ آ The first word out was ï؟½parallel,ï؟½ and seven let it be,
آ آ آ آ Till Joe waltzed in his ï؟½double lï؟½ betwixt the ï؟½aï؟½ and ï؟½e;ï؟½
آ آ آ آ For since he drilled them Mexicans in San Jacintoï؟½s fight
آ آ آ آ Thar warnï؟½t no prouder man got up than Pistol Joe that night
آ آ آ آ Till ï؟½rhythmï؟½ came! He tried to smile, then said ï؟½they had him there,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And Lanky Jim, with one long stride, got up and took his chair.

آ آ آ آ O little kids, my pretty kids, ï؟½twas touchinï؟½ to survey
آ آ آ آ These bearded men, with weppings on, like schoolboys at their play.
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½d laugh with glee, and shout to see each other lead the van,
آ آ آ آ And Bob sat up as monitor with a cue for a rattan,
آ آ آ آ Till the Chair gave out ï؟½incinerate,ï؟½ and Brown said heï؟½d be durned
آ آ آ آ If any such blamed word as that in school was ever learned.

آ آ آ آ When ï؟½phthisisï؟½ came they all sprang up, and vowed the man who rung
آ آ آ آ Another blamed Greek word on them be taken out and hung.
آ آ آ آ As they sat down again I saw in Bilsonï؟½s eye a flash,
آ آ آ آ And Brown of Calaveras was a-twistinï؟½ his mustache,
آ آ آ آ And when at last Brown slipped on ï؟½gneiss,ï؟½ and Bilson took his chair,
آ آ آ آ He dropped some casual words about some folks who dyed their hair.

آ آ آ آ And then the Chair grew very white, and the Chair said heï؟½d adjourn,
آ آ آ آ But Poker Dick remarked that he would wait and get his turn;
آ آ آ آ Then with a tremblinï؟½ voice and hand, and with a wanderinï؟½ eye,
آ آ آ آ The Chair next offered ï؟½eider-duck,ï؟½ and Dick began with ï؟½Iï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And Bilson smiled then Bilson shrieked! Just how the fight begun
آ آ آ آ I never knowed, for Bilson dropped, and Dick, he moved up one.

آ آ آ آ Then certain gents arose and said ï؟½theyï؟½d business down in camp,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½ez the road was rather dark, and ez the night was damp,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½dï؟½ here got up Three-fingered Jack and locked the door and yelled:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½No, not one motherï؟½s son goes out till that thar word is spelled!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ But while the words were on his lips, he groaned and sank in pain,
آ آ آ آ And sank with Webster on his chest and Worcester on his brain.

آ آ آ آ Below the bar dodged Poker Dick, and tried to look ez he
آ آ آ آ Was huntinï؟½ up authorities thet no one else could see;
آ آ آ آ And Brown got down behind the stove, allowinï؟½ he ï؟½was cold,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Till it upsot and down his legs the cinders freely rolled,
آ آ آ آ And several gents called ï؟½Order!ï؟½ till in his simple way
آ آ آ آ Poor Smith began with ï؟½O-rï؟½ ï؟½Orï؟½ and he was dragged away.

آ آ آ آ O little kids, my pretty kids, down on your knees and pray!
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½ve got your eddication in a peaceful sort of way;
آ آ آ آ And bear in mind thar may be sharps ez slings their spellinï؟½ square,
آ آ آ آ But likewise slings their bowie-knives without a thought or care.
آ آ آ آ You wants to know the rest, my dears? Thetï؟½s all! In me you see
آ آ آ آ The only gent that lived to tell about the Spellinï؟½ Bee!

آ آ آ آ He ceased and passed, that truthful man; the children went their way
آ آ آ آ With downcast heads and downcast hearts but not to sport or play.
آ آ آ آ For when at eve the lamps were lit, and supperless to bed
آ آ آ آ Each child was sent, with tasks undone and lessons all unsaid,
آ آ آ آ No man might know the awful woe that thrilled their youthful frames,
آ آ آ آ As they dreamed of Angels Spelling Bee and thought of Truthful James.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
